text: The surgeon injects vasopressin at the junction of the uterus and a large myoma, approximately four times the size of the small nulliparous uterus. The myoma exhibits a marble white appearance due to transient ischemia caused by the vasopressin injection.

On the anterior surface of the uterus, the surgeon performs another injection of vasopressin, initiating hydrodissection. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut the anterior seromuscular layer of the myoma, minimizing bleeding and creating a clear dissection plane. A myoma screw, suction irrigation, and a grasper are employed to manipulate the tissue and further define the dissection window.

The surgeon identifies the capsule of the myoma and enucleates it from the uterus using two myoma screws for traction. Two-thirds of the myoma is visibly separated from the capsule, with careful manipulation to avoid avulsion of the endometrial layer. The myoma screw is relocated to assist in the complete removal of the myoma without opening the uterine cavity.

The surgeon completes the enucleation of the myoma, fully removing it from the uterus. A myoma screw is detached, and suction irrigation is performed. The surgeon begins closing the uterus using an extra corporeal western knot, followed by continuous suturing with a dandy jamming knot. Multiple layers of sutures are applied to achieve hemostasis and obliterate dead space, ensuring no bleeding occurs.

The surgeon addresses minor bleeding by applying multiple sutures, effectively stopping the bleeding. A posterior wall myoma, approximately 1.5 to 2 centimeters in size, is identified and removed. The surgeon uses extra corporeal western knots for suturing, ensuring secure closure with minimal blood loss. The dandy jamming knot technique is employed for continuous suturing, providing additional layers of safety to prevent future uterine rupture.

The surgeon applies an interseed to prevent adhesions, followed by continuous interlocked blanket suturing on the uterus. The assistant lifts the suture towards the anterior wall for tension. The uterus regains its normal color and size, indicating successful removal of all myomas. An extracorporeal square knot is used to secure a bleeder point at the fundus, with careful bites to protect the tubal ostea. The Clark knot pusher assists in tying the knot, ensuring hemostasis and strengthening the uterus. Irrigation and suction clear the surgical field, leaving the pelvis with clear fluid and minimal bleeding.

The surgeon completes the morselation of the myoma, using stone holding forceps to carefully remove morselated tissue particles from the body. A thorough lavage is performed to clean all quadrants of the abdomen with suction. Interseed, a deoxygenated cellulose material, is spread over the suture area as a mechanical adhesive barrier to prevent adhesions.
